cases.title: Don't Be Distracted by the Obvious
cases.presentation: A motorcyclist has a grotesquely angulated open tibia fracture with bone exposed. He is also pale, anxious, and breathing fast. Your new partner kneels to splint the leg immediately.
cases.question: What is the correct priority?
options: Splint the dramatic leg fracture first
Complete the Primary Survey for life threats and treat shock; control bleeding, then address the fracture
Apply a traction splint before assessing ABCs
cases.explanation: A dramatic extremity injury must not distract from life threats. Tachycardia, pallor, tachypnea, and a narrowing pulse pressure suggest shock—complete the Primary Survey, control hemorrhage, and treat for shock. The open fracture is covered and managed after life threats.
cases.clinicalPearl: The most eye-catching injury is rarely the most lethal—finish the Primary Survey before splinting.

cases.title: Pulseless Angulated Forearm
cases.presentation: A teenager fell off a skateboard with a severely angulated forearm. The hand is pale and cool, and you cannot palpate a radial pulse distal to the deformity. Capillary refill is delayed.
cases.question: What is the appropriate management?
options: Splint exactly as found and transport
Apply gentle in-line traction to realign and attempt to restore distal circulation, then splint and recheck the pulse
Apply a tourniquet above the elbow
cases.explanation: An absent distal pulse with a pale, cool hand is limb-threatening ischemia. Gentle in-line traction to realign the angulated fracture may restore circulation; then splint and recheck PMS. If the pulse does not return, splint and transport rapidly.
cases.clinicalPearl: A pulseless, angulated long-bone fracture warrants gentle realignment to restore perfusion—then recheck and document the pulse.

cases.title: Prolonged Entrapment and Crush
cases.presentation: A worker's legs were pinned under a collapsed wall for over two hours. Heavy machinery is about to free him. He is alert with crushed, insensate legs.
cases.question: What is the key concern as the crushing force is released?
options: Crush syndrome with hyperkalemia and dysrhythmias—coordinate fluid resuscitation and monitoring with medical direction before/at release
cases.explanation: Prolonged crush causes rhabdomyolysis; releasing the compression washes potassium and myoglobin into the circulation, risking hyperkalemia, lethal dysrhythmias, and renal failure (crush syndrome). Establish IV access, begin fluids, apply cardiac monitoring, and coordinate management with medical direction around the moment of release.
cases.clinicalPearl: The dangerous moment in a prolonged crush is the release—pre-treat with fluids and monitoring for hyperkalemia per medical direction.
